---
title: "The Bayesian Turn: Probability as Degree of Belief"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Statistical Inference"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

rng = np.random.default_rng(20240920)

PRIOR  = "#6c757d"     # the prior
LIKE   = "#0a7d4d"     # the likelihood (as a function of theta)
POST   = "#2b3a67"     # the posterior
TRUTH  = "#c1121f"     # the true parameter / point estimates
FILL   = "#9fb3d1"     # shaded credible mass
ACCENT = "#d6791f"     # secondary highlights
GREY   = "#9aa0a6"
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the **closing article** of the Statistical
> Inference track — the fork in the road. Every article before it answered the charter
> question, *"what can we conclude from data, and how sure are we,"* inside the
> **frequentist** frame, where the parameter is a fixed unknown and the data are random.
> This article keeps the question and changes the frame: the parameter becomes the random
> thing, and "how sure are we" becomes a literal probability. It assumes three earlier
> articles directly. From [INFR-B1 *The Likelihood*] it takes the likelihood
> $L(\theta)=p(x\mid\theta)$ as the object that scores how well each parameter explains the
> data — the Bayesian posterior is *this object times a prior*. From [INFR-C1 *Confidence
> Intervals*] it takes the carefully-built notion of a confidence interval, against which
> we contrast the **credible interval** (the payoff of the whole track's framing). From
> [INFR-D1 *The Logic of Hypothesis Testing*] it takes the testing apparatus we re-answer
> with posterior probabilities. From the Probability Foundations track it borrows, each
> built there from the ground up, **Bayes' rule for events** ([PROB-06]) and the **Beta and
> Gamma distributions** ([PROB-14]); we import only their statements. Nothing else above the
> Calculus-I floor is assumed. This article **sets up** a future *Bayesian Computation /
> MCMC* track (which builds the machinery for the non-conjugate case we can only gesture at
> here) and closes a loop back to **Regression** by showing that the MAP estimate under a
> Gaussian or Laplace prior *is* ridge or lasso.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Frequentist inference treats the parameter $\theta$ as a fixed unknown constant and the
data as random; "$95\%$ confident" is a property of a *procedure*, never of the one
interval in your hands. The **Bayesian turn** is a single, consequential change of
definition: treat probability as a **degree of belief**, let $\theta$ itself be a random
variable carrying a distribution, and then the data update that distribution by the same
Bayes' rule you already know for events. This article derives **Bayes' theorem for
parameters** — $\text{posterior}\propto\text{likelihood}\times\text{prior}$ — from
nothing but the definition of conditional density, names every piece (the prior, the
likelihood, and the marginal likelihood that makes it integrate to one), and carries two
**conjugate** models all the way through by hand: the **Beta–Binomial** (a coin's bias)
and the **Normal–Normal** (a mean with known variance). With a full posterior in hand we
build the **credible interval** and place it beside the confidence interval, making the
philosophical and numerical difference concrete: the credible interval *does* license the
sentence "there is a $95\%$ probability $\theta$ is in here" that the confidence interval
forbids. We derive the **MAP** estimate, show it is the posterior's mode, prove it reduces
to the MLE under a flat prior, and prove that under Gaussian and Laplace priors it is
exactly **ridge** and **lasso** regression — the link back to the Regression track. Then
the honest boundary: prior sensitivity, when Bayes and frequentism agree and when they
diverge, and the computational price (the marginal likelihood is an integral that is
usually intractable, which is why a whole MCMC track must follow). The single claim the
article defends is that **the Bayesian and frequentist answers to "how sure are we" are
different objects answering different questions, and a graduate must be able to state
precisely which one a given problem calls for.**

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Make one change to everything that came before: treat probability as a **degree of
belief**, so that the unknown parameter $\theta$ is itself a **random variable** with a
distribution. Before seeing data you hold a **prior** $\pi(\theta)$ — what you believe
about $\theta$ in advance. The data arrive through the **likelihood** $p(x\mid\theta)$ from
[INFR-B1]. Bayes' rule combines them into the **posterior** — your updated belief about
$\theta$ *after* seeing the data:
$$
\underbrace{p(\theta\mid x)}_{\text{posterior}}
\;=\;
\frac{\overbrace{p(x\mid\theta)}^{\text{likelihood}}\;\overbrace{\pi(\theta)}^{\text{prior}}}
     {\underbrace{\textstyle\int p(x\mid\theta')\,\pi(\theta')\,d\theta'}_{\text{marginal likelihood } p(x)}}
\;\;\propto\;\; p(x\mid\theta)\,\pi(\theta).
$$
The denominator is a constant in $\theta$ (the data are fixed); it only rescales the
numerator so the posterior integrates to one. So the working slogan is
$$
\boxed{\;\text{posterior} \;\propto\; \text{likelihood} \times \text{prior}.\;}
$$
Everything Bayesian is a reading of this one distribution. A **point estimate** is its
mode (the **MAP**) or its mean. A **credible interval** is a region holding $95\%$ of its
mass — and unlike a confidence interval, it *does* mean "there is a $95\%$ probability
$\theta$ is in here," because here $\theta$ is genuinely random. A **test** compares the
posterior probabilities of competing hypotheses. The price: that denominator is an
integral, and outside the **conjugate** cases we work by hand below, it is usually
intractable — which is the entire reason a Bayesian-computation track must follow.
:::

Everything in this article orbits that box. The hinge is the redefinition of probability
itself, so we spend the next section on *why* anyone would make that move and *what it buys*
— because the whole track until now was built on the opposite definition, and the reader is
owed a clear account of what is being traded.

# Why we are doing this: the problem that forced the turn {#sec-why}

Hold one question in view for the entire article: **what changes when we let the parameter
be random, and how does that re-answer the same "how sure are we" question the whole track
has been chasing?** Every derivation below answers a piece of that.

## The itch the frequentist interval never scratched

Go back to [INFR-C1]. We built a $95\%$ confidence interval and then spent an entire section
forbidding the one interpretation everyone's intuition reaches for: *"there's a $95\%$
chance the parameter is in this interval."* That sentence is false in the frequentist frame
for a precise reason — once the data are in, the interval is a fixed pair of numbers and
$\theta$ is a fixed number, so the statement "$L\le\theta\le U$" is simply true or false,
with no probability left to attach. The $95\%$ described the *procedure*, not the interval.

This is correct, and it is also deeply unsatisfying. The question a scientist actually has
is: *given the data I collected, how probable is it that the drug works / the effect is
positive / the rate is below the safety threshold?* The frequentist apparatus cannot answer
that question **as stated**, because it refuses to put a probability on $\theta$ at all.
It answers a subtly different question — *how would this procedure behave over many
hypothetical repetitions?* — and asks you to be satisfied with that.

The Bayesian turn is the decision to answer the question *as asked*. To say "there is a
$95\%$ probability $\theta$ lies in $[a,b]$" and mean it, you must be willing to treat
$\theta$ as a random variable — something that *can* carry a probability. That single
willingness is the entire move. Everything else is consequence.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is the Bayesian approach 'more correct' than the frequentist one?"* No — and answering
"yes" is a tell that you do not understand either. They answer **different questions** under
**different definitions of probability**. Frequentist probability is a long-run frequency:
it is defined only for things that *can* be repeated, so it attaches to procedures and data,
never to a one-off fixed constant like "the true mean income in 2026." Bayesian probability
is a degree of belief: it attaches to *any* proposition whose truth is uncertain to you,
including a one-off constant. Each is internally coherent. The mature answer is: the
frequentist guarantee is about *calibration over repetition* and needs no prior; the
Bayesian answer is a *direct probability about this case* and needs a prior. Which you want
depends on whether your question is "how does my method behave in the long run" or "what
should I believe right now, given these data and what I knew before."
:::

## A two-paragraph history

The degree-of-belief view is in fact the *older* one. Thomas Bayes' essay, published
posthumously in 1763, and Pierre-Simon Laplace's far more developed work around 1774–1812,
treated probability as a state of knowledge and updated it with data exactly as we will
below — Laplace used it to estimate the mass of Saturn and famously to ask the probability
that the sun would rise tomorrow. For over a century this "inverse probability" *was*
statistics.

The frequentist framework — Fisher's likelihood, Neyman and Pearson's tests and confidence
intervals — was a deliberate early-20th-century *reaction* against putting probabilities on
hypotheses, motivated by discomfort with the apparent arbitrariness of the prior. It won
the textbooks for most of the century. The Bayesian view never died (Jeffreys, Savage, de
Finetti kept it rigorous and gave the degree-of-belief idea axiomatic foundations), and it
returned to dominance once computers could evaluate the awkward integral in the Core box by
simulation — the MCMC revolution of the late 1980s and 1990s. So the turn we make here is
not a fringe alternative; it is half of the field, and the half that answers the question
practitioners actually ask.

# Intuition before symbols: belief as a reweighting {#sec-intuition}

Before any algebra, here is the picture that makes the posterior unsurprising. You start
with a belief about $\theta$ spread across its possible values — the prior, a curve over the
$\theta$-axis. The data do not *replace* that belief; they **reweight** it. The likelihood
$L(\theta)=p(x\mid\theta)$, read as a function of $\theta$ (exactly the backward reading of
[INFR-B1]), is tall at the $\theta$ values that explain the data well and short where they
explain it badly. Multiply the prior curve by the likelihood curve, point by point, and you
get the posterior (up to rescaling): belief survives only where it was *both* plausible a
priori *and* consistent with the data.

@fig-update draws one such update for a coin. The flat-ish prior says "I don't know the
bias." The likelihood from seeing 9 heads in 12 flips leans right. The posterior is the
product — pulled toward the data but still carrying a memory of the prior.

```{python}
#| label: fig-update
#| fig-cap: 'Bayesian updating as reweighting, for a coin''s bias $\theta$. The prior (grey) is a mild belief centred at a fair coin. The likelihood (green), as a function of $\theta$ after observing 9 heads in 12 flips, peaks near $\theta=0.75$. The posterior (navy) is their pointwise product, renormalised to integrate to one: belief survives only where the prior and the data agree. The posterior sits between the prior''s centre and the data''s, and is narrower than either input — combining two sources of information sharpens belief.'
theta = np.linspace(0, 1, 500)

# A mild Beta(2,2) prior: believes the coin is roughly fair, weakly.
a0, b0 = 2.0, 2.0
prior = stats.beta(a0, b0).pdf(theta)

# Data: 9 heads in 12 flips. Likelihood as a function of theta (up to a constant).
k, n = 9, 12
like = theta**k * (1 - theta)**(n - k)
like_scaled = like / np.trapezoid(like, theta)   # scale only so it is visible on the same axes

# Posterior is Beta(a0 + k, b0 + n - k) — derived in the text below.
post = stats.beta(a0 + k, b0 + n - k).pdf(theta)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(theta, prior, color=PRIOR, lw=2.0, label="prior  $\\pi(\\theta)=$ Beta(2,2)")
ax.plot(theta, like_scaled, color=LIKE, lw=2.0, ls="--",
        label="likelihood (rescaled)")
ax.plot(theta, post, color=POST, lw=2.4, label="posterior  Beta(11,5)")
ax.fill_between(theta, post, color=FILL, alpha=0.35)
ax.set_xlabel(r"coin bias $\theta$")
ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("Posterior $\\propto$ likelihood $\\times$ prior")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Where does the prior come from, and isn't choosing it cheating?"* The prior encodes what
you knew before the data — past studies, physical constraints (a probability must lie in
$[0,1]$), expert judgment, or a deliberate statement of ignorance. It is not cheating; it is
**explicit**, which is its virtue and its liability. The virtue: every assumption is on the
page and auditable. The liability: a different analyst may choose differently, so honest
Bayesian work *reports prior sensitivity* (we do exactly this in @sec-sensitivity). The
frequentist who says "I used no prior" has in effect used a particular one (a flat prior,
as we prove in @sec-map) and simply not labelled it. The interview-grade point: the choice
is unavoidable; the Bayesian merely makes it visible.
:::

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## From Bayes' rule for events to Bayes' rule for parameters {#sec-bayes-derive}

We build the Core box from the ground up. The only import is Bayes' rule for events, which
the Probability track ([PROB-06]) derives in one line from the definition of conditional
probability; we restate it so the derivation is self-contained. For two events $A$ and $B$
with $P(B)>0$,
$$
P(A\mid B) \;=\; \frac{P(B\mid A)\,P(A)}{P(B)},
$$ {#eq-bayes-events}
which follows because $P(A\mid B)P(B)=P(A\cap B)=P(B\mid A)P(A)$ — two ways of writing the
same joint probability — and dividing by $P(B)$ isolates $P(A\mid B)$.

Now we promote this from events to a continuous parameter and continuous data. This is the
step that is *not* in the Probability track, so we do every move. Let $\theta$ be the
unknown parameter, now modelled as a random variable with a **prior density** $\pi(\theta)$,
and let $x$ denote the observed data with **conditional density** $p(x\mid\theta)$ (the
model — the same forward-facing object from [INFR-B1]). The **joint density** of parameter
and data is, by the definition of conditional density (the continuous analogue of
$P(A\cap B)=P(B\mid A)P(A)$),
$$
p(\theta, x) \;=\; p(x\mid\theta)\,\pi(\theta).
$$ {#eq-joint}
We can equally factor the joint the other way, conditioning on the data instead:
$$
p(\theta, x) \;=\; p(\theta\mid x)\,p(x),
$$ {#eq-joint-other}
where $p(\theta\mid x)$ is the **posterior density** we want and $p(x)$ is the
**marginal density of the data** — the density of $x$ with $\theta$ integrated out.
Setting @eq-joint equal to @eq-joint-other (both equal the one joint density) and solving
for the posterior:
$$
p(\theta\mid x)\,p(x) \;=\; p(x\mid\theta)\,\pi(\theta)
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
p(\theta\mid x) \;=\; \frac{p(x\mid\theta)\,\pi(\theta)}{p(x)}.
$$ {#eq-bayes-param}

That is **Bayes' theorem for parameters**, derived from nothing but the definition of a
conditional density used twice. It is the exact analogue of @eq-bayes-events with the
substitutions $A\to\theta$, $B\to x$, and probabilities of events replaced by densities.

The denominator $p(x)$ needs unpacking, because it is the piece that is both essential and
troublesome. By the **law of total probability** in its continuous form — sum (integrate)
the joint density over all possible values of the conditioned-out variable —
$$
p(x) \;=\; \int p(\theta, x)\,d\theta \;=\; \int p(x\mid\theta')\,\pi(\theta')\,d\theta'.
$$ {#eq-marginal}
(We use a dummy variable $\theta'$ inside the integral to keep it distinct from the
$\theta$ on the left of @eq-bayes-param; the integral runs over *all* parameter values and
returns a number that no longer depends on $\theta$.) This $p(x)$ is variously called the
**marginal likelihood**, the **evidence**, or the **normalizing constant**.

Here is the single most useful observation about it: **with the data $x$ fixed, $p(x)$ is a
constant in $\theta$.** It does not change the *shape* of the posterior as a function of
$\theta$ — it only scales it so the total area is one. Therefore, for everything except
problems that require the actual normalized density, we may drop it and write
$$
p(\theta\mid x) \;\propto\; p(x\mid\theta)\,\pi(\theta)
\;=\; L(\theta)\,\pi(\theta),
$$ {#eq-proportional}
the proportionality slogan of the Core, where $L(\theta)=p(x\mid\theta)$ is precisely the
likelihood of [INFR-B1]. The $\propto$ sign carries real meaning: *the posterior is the
likelihood-times-prior curve, rescaled by whatever constant makes it integrate to one.*

::: {.callout-important title="Why the proportionality form is the one you actually use"}
The full Bayes formula @eq-bayes-param looks like it demands the hard integral @eq-marginal.
But for *estimation* — finding the posterior's mode, mean, or a credible interval — the
constant $p(x)$ never matters, because it scales the whole curve equally. We will exploit
this repeatedly: recognize the *shape* of $L(\theta)\pi(\theta)$ as a known distribution,
and read off its normalizing constant for free. That trick is exactly what makes the
**conjugate** examples below solvable by hand. The integral @eq-marginal only bites when you
need $p(x)$ itself — for **model comparison** (Bayes factors) or when the shape is *not*
recognizable, which is the non-conjugate world that forces simulation.
:::

## Naming the four pieces {#sec-four-pieces}

Each object in @eq-bayes-param earns a name, and an interview will probe whether you know
what each *is* and *is not*.

- **Prior $\pi(\theta)$** — a genuine probability density over $\theta$, integrating to one,
  encoding belief *before* the data. It is a density over the *parameter*, which is the
  thing the likelihood emphatically was *not* ([INFR-B1] proved $L(\theta)$ need not
  integrate to one). This is the conceptual novelty.
- **Likelihood $p(x\mid\theta)=L(\theta)$** — the model read backwards, exactly as in
  [INFR-B1]. As a function of $\theta$ it is *not* a density (no normalization in $\theta$);
  it is the data's verdict on each $\theta$.
- **Posterior $p(\theta\mid x)$** — a genuine probability density over $\theta$ (the prior,
  updated). This is the whole output of a Bayesian analysis; every Bayesian quantity is a
  summary of it.
- **Marginal likelihood / evidence $p(x)$** — a single number once the data are fixed: the
  prior-averaged probability of the data. It normalizes the posterior and scores the model
  as a whole.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"In one breath, what is the difference between the likelihood and the posterior?"* The
likelihood $p(x\mid\theta)$ is a function of $\theta$ that is **not** a probability
distribution over $\theta$ — it ranks parameters by fit but does not integrate to one and
carries no prior belief. The posterior $p(\theta\mid x)$ **is** a probability distribution
over $\theta$ — it is the likelihood multiplied by the prior and renormalized. The MLE
maximizes the first; the MAP maximizes the second; they coincide exactly when the prior is
flat (@sec-map). If you can say that cleanly, you have the spine of the article.
:::

## Conjugacy: when the posterior stays in the family {#sec-conjugacy}

The integral @eq-marginal is the villain of Bayesian computation. For a special and
beautiful class of prior–likelihood pairs it disappears entirely, because the posterior
turns out to be the *same kind of distribution* as the prior, just with updated parameters.
We can then read the answer off by pattern-matching, no integral required.

::: {#def-conjugate}
**Definition (conjugate prior).** A family $\mathcal F$ of prior distributions is
**conjugate** to a likelihood $p(x\mid\theta)$ if, whenever the prior $\pi(\theta)$ is in
$\mathcal F$, the posterior $p(\theta\mid x)$ is also in $\mathcal F$ — only its parameters
change.
:::

Conjugacy is a computational convenience, not a law of nature — but it is the cleanest
possible window onto *how* the data move belief, because we can watch the prior's parameters
update in closed form. We work the two canonical cases completely.

## Worked conjugate case I: the Beta–Binomial (a coin's bias) {#sec-beta-binom}

The setting is the most basic in statistics: we flip a coin (or run a click-through test,
or count survivals) $n$ times, see $k$ "successes," and want to learn the success
probability $\theta\in[0,1]$. The likelihood is Binomial; with $k$ successes in $n$
independent Bernoulli$(\theta)$ trials,
$$
p(k\mid\theta) \;=\; \binom{n}{k}\,\theta^{k}\,(1-\theta)^{\,n-k}.
$$ {#eq-binom-like}
As a function of $\theta$ (data fixed), the binomial coefficient $\binom{n}{k}$ is a
constant, so $L(\theta)\propto \theta^{k}(1-\theta)^{n-k}$.

We need a prior on $\theta\in[0,1]$. The **Beta distribution** is the natural choice — it
lives on exactly that interval. (The Beta is built from first principles in [PROB-14]; we
import only its density.) With shape parameters $a,b>0$,
$$
\pi(\theta) \;=\; \frac{1}{B(a,b)}\,\theta^{a-1}(1-\theta)^{b-1},
\qquad 0\le\theta\le 1,
$$ {#eq-beta-prior}
where $B(a,b)=\int_0^1 t^{a-1}(1-t)^{b-1}\,dt$ is the **Beta function**, the constant that
makes @eq-beta-prior integrate to one. Notice the shape: a power of $\theta$ times a power
of $(1-\theta)$ — the *same algebraic form* as the binomial likelihood. That is the whole
reason conjugacy will work.

Now multiply, using the proportionality form @eq-proportional and dropping every factor
that does not contain $\theta$ (the $\binom{n}{k}$ and the $1/B(a,b)$ are both constants in
$\theta$):
$$
p(\theta\mid k)
\;\propto\; L(\theta)\,\pi(\theta)
\;\propto\; \underbrace{\theta^{k}(1-\theta)^{n-k}}_{\text{likelihood}}\;\cdot\;
            \underbrace{\theta^{a-1}(1-\theta)^{b-1}}_{\text{prior}}.
$$
Combine the powers by adding exponents (the rule $\theta^{p}\theta^{q}=\theta^{p+q}$ from
Calc-1-level algebra):
$$
p(\theta\mid k)
\;\propto\; \theta^{\,(a+k)-1}\,(1-\theta)^{\,(b+n-k)-1}.
$$ {#eq-beta-post-shape}

Look hard at @eq-beta-post-shape and compare it to the Beta density @eq-beta-prior. It is
**exactly the algebraic form of a Beta**, with new parameters
$$
\boxed{\;a' = a + k, \qquad b' = b + (n - k).\;}
$$ {#eq-beta-update}
There is only one normalized density with that shape — the $\mathrm{Beta}(a',b')$ density —
so the proportionality must in fact be an equality once we attach the correct constant
$1/B(a',b')$. We did not have to *compute* any integral: we recognized the shape and the
normalizer is forced. The posterior is
$$
\theta\mid k \;\sim\; \mathrm{Beta}\bigl(a + k,\; b + n - k\bigr).
$$ {#eq-beta-post}

This is the entire Beta–Binomial update, and it is worth reading for meaning. The prior
parameters $(a,b)$ behave exactly like **prior pseudo-counts**: $a$ acts as "successes seen
in imagination before any data," $b$ as imagined failures. The data add real counts: $k$
successes onto $a$, and $n-k$ failures onto $b$. The Beta$(2,2)$ prior of @fig-update was
"pretend I saw 2 heads and 2 tails"; after 9 real heads and 3 real tails the posterior is
Beta$(2+9,\,2+3)=\mathrm{Beta}(11,5)$, exactly the navy curve there.

The posterior **mean** has a clean closed form (the mean of a Beta$(a',b')$ is
$a'/(a'+b')$, a fact from [PROB-14]):
$$
\mathbb E[\theta\mid k]
= \frac{a + k}{a + b + n}.
$$ {#eq-beta-mean}
Rearrange it to expose the most important idea in Bayesian estimation — that the posterior
is a **compromise** between prior and data. Write the data's own estimate (the MLE) as
$\hat\theta_{\text{MLE}}=k/n$ and the prior mean as $\theta_0 = a/(a+b)$. Then, multiplying
out,
$$
\frac{a+k}{a+b+n}
= \frac{a+b}{a+b+n}\cdot\frac{a}{a+b}
   \;+\; \frac{n}{a+b+n}\cdot\frac{k}{n}
= \underbrace{w}_{\text{prior weight}}\theta_0
   \;+\; \underbrace{(1-w)}_{\text{data weight}}\hat\theta_{\text{MLE}},
$$ {#eq-shrinkage}
where $w=(a+b)/(a+b+n)$. (Check the algebra: the first term's numerator is
$(a+b)\cdot\frac{a}{a+b}=a$ over $a+b+n$, and the second is $n\cdot\frac{k}{n}=k$ over
$a+b+n$; adding gives $(a+k)/(a+b+n)$, matching @eq-beta-mean.) So the posterior mean is a
**weighted average** of the prior mean and the data mean, with weights set by the
"strength" $a+b$ of the prior versus the sample size $n$. As $n\to\infty$ the data weight
$1-w\to 1$ and the posterior mean converges to the MLE: **the data eventually overwhelm any
fixed prior.** This convergence is the formal sense in which Bayes and frequentism agree in
the large-sample limit, taken up in @sec-agree.

```{python}
#| label: fig-beta-sequence
#| fig-cap: 'The Beta-Binomial posterior sharpening as data accumulate, all from the same Beta(2,2) prior. A coin with true bias $\theta=0.7$ (red line) is flipped; after $n=5,20,80,320$ flips the posterior (navy, progressively darker) concentrates ever more tightly around the truth, and its mean (the weighted average of @eq-shrinkage) marches from the prior''s 0.5 toward 0.7. With little data the prior still pulls the estimate toward a fair coin; with much data the prior is irrelevant. This is Bayesian learning in one picture.'
theta = np.linspace(0, 1, 500)
a0, b0 = 2.0, 2.0
true_p = 0.7
ns = [5, 20, 80, 320]
shades = ["#9fb3d1", "#6f86b3", "#41598c", "#1f2f55"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(theta, stats.beta(a0, b0).pdf(theta), color=PRIOR, lw=2.0,
        ls=":", label="prior Beta(2,2)")
# One growing data stream so the updates are nested.
flips = (rng.random(max(ns)) < true_p).astype(int)
for n, c in zip(ns, shades):
    k = int(flips[:n].sum())
    post = stats.beta(a0 + k, b0 + n - k)
    ax.plot(theta, post.pdf(theta), color=c, lw=2.2,
            label=f"n={n}: Beta({a0+k:.0f},{b0+n-k:.0f}), mean {post.mean():.3f}")
ax.axvline(true_p, color=TRUTH, lw=2.0, label=r"true $\theta=0.7$")
ax.set_xlabel(r"coin bias $\theta$"); ax.set_ylabel("posterior density")
ax.set_title("Posterior concentrates on the truth as data grow")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What prior means 'I know nothing'?"* For the coin, $\mathrm{Beta}(1,1)$ is the
**uniform** prior on $[0,1]$ — its density is $\theta^{0}(1-\theta)^{0}/B(1,1)=1$, flat
everywhere. Plug $a=b=1$ into @eq-beta-mean: the posterior mean becomes
$(1+k)/(2+n)$, the **Laplace rule of succession** (Laplace's answer to "will the sun rise
tomorrow"). With a flat prior the posterior is $\propto L(\theta)$, so the posterior *mode*
is the MLE $k/n$ — a first glimpse of @sec-map. But beware the word "uninformative":
$\mathrm{Beta}(1,1)$ is flat in $\theta$, yet *not* flat in, say, the log-odds of $\theta$ —
"knowing nothing" is not reparameterization-invariant. The Jeffreys prior $\mathrm{Beta}(½,
½)$ is the principled answer to that subtlety; it is built from the Fisher information of
[INFR-B3], and we point to it in @sec-deeper rather than derive it here.
:::

## Worked conjugate case II: the Normal–Normal (a mean, known variance) {#sec-normal-normal}

The second canonical case mirrors the confidence-interval setting of [INFR-C1] so we can
later place the credible interval directly beside the confidence interval on the *same*
problem. We observe $x_1,\dots,x_n$ i.i.d. $\mathcal N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ with the variance
$\sigma^2$ **known** (the same honest simplification as [INFR-C1]; it isolates the idea),
and we want the mean $\mu$.

The likelihood, as a function of $\mu$, comes from the Gaussian density. Multiplying the $n$
independent densities and dropping every factor free of $\mu$:
$$
L(\mu)
\;\propto\; \exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i-\mu)^2\Bigr).
$$
Expand the sum and isolate $\mu$. Using $\sum(x_i-\mu)^2=\sum(x_i-\bar x)^2 +
n(\bar x-\mu)^2$ (the standard centering identity — add and subtract $\bar x$; the cross
term vanishes because $\sum(x_i-\bar x)=0$), the first piece is constant in $\mu$, so
$$
L(\mu)
\;\propto\; \exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{n}{2\sigma^2}(\mu-\bar x)^2\Bigr).
$$ {#eq-normal-like}
Read as a function of $\mu$, this is **itself the shape of a Gaussian**. To see it, match
the exponent to the canonical Gaussian exponent $-\tfrac{1}{2v}(\mu-c)^2$, which has centre
$c$ and variance $v$: writing $\tfrac{n}{2\sigma^2}=\tfrac{1}{2(\sigma^2/n)}$ shows the
exponent is $-\tfrac{1}{2(\sigma^2/n)}(\mu-\bar x)^2$, so $L(\mu)$ has the shape of a Gaussian
in $\mu$ centred at $c=\bar x$ with variance $v=\sigma^2/n$. (That variance $\sigma^2/n$ is
exactly the squared standard error from [INFR-A1].) This is why the Gaussian is
self-conjugate.

Take a **Gaussian prior** for $\mu$, centred at our prior guess $\mu_0$ with prior variance
$\tau_0^2$ (large $\tau_0^2$ = vague prior, small = confident):
$$
\pi(\mu)\;\propto\;\exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{1}{2\tau_0^2}(\mu-\mu_0)^2\Bigr).
$$ {#eq-normal-prior}
Multiply prior and likelihood; the posterior is proportional to the product of two
exponentials, so we add the exponents:
$$
p(\mu\mid x)
\;\propto\;
\exp\!\Bigl(-\tfrac12\Bigl[\tfrac{n}{\sigma^2}(\mu-\bar x)^2
                          +\tfrac{1}{\tau_0^2}(\mu-\mu_0)^2\Bigr]\Bigr).
$$ {#eq-nn-product}
The bracket is a quadratic in $\mu$, so the posterior is again a Gaussian — we only need to
find its centre and width by **completing the square**. Write the bracket as
$Q(\mu)=A(\mu-\bar x)^2 + B(\mu-\mu_0)^2$ with $A=n/\sigma^2$ and $B=1/\tau_0^2$ (these are
called **precisions** — inverse variances; the names will pay off). Expand both squares:
$$
Q(\mu) = A\mu^2 - 2A\bar x\,\mu + A\bar x^2
       + B\mu^2 - 2B\mu_0\,\mu + B\mu_0^2 .
$$
Collect the $\mu^2$, $\mu$, and constant terms:
$$
Q(\mu) = (A+B)\mu^2 \;-\; 2(A\bar x + B\mu_0)\,\mu \;+\;\text{(const in }\mu).
$$
Factor out $(A+B)$ from the first two terms and complete the square:
$$
Q(\mu) = (A+B)\Bigl[\mu^2 - 2\,\frac{A\bar x + B\mu_0}{A+B}\,\mu\Bigr] + \text{const}
       = (A+B)\Bigl(\mu - \frac{A\bar x + B\mu_0}{A+B}\Bigr)^2 + \text{const}',
$$
where the constants absorb everything free of $\mu$ (they vanish into the proportionality).
Matching this to the exponent of a Gaussian $\exp(-\tfrac{1}{2\tau_n^2}(\mu-\mu_n)^2)$, where
the exponent is $-\tfrac12\cdot\tfrac{1}{\tau_n^2}(\mu-\mu_n)^2$, we read off the
**posterior precision** $1/\tau_n^2 = A+B$ and the **posterior mean** $\mu_n=(A\bar x +
B\mu_0)/(A+B)$. Substituting $A=n/\sigma^2$ and $B=1/\tau_0^2$ back:
$$
\boxed{\;
\frac{1}{\tau_n^2} = \frac{n}{\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{\tau_0^2},
\qquad
\mu_n = \tau_n^2\Bigl(\frac{n\,\bar x}{\sigma^2} + \frac{\mu_0}{\tau_0^2}\Bigr).
\;}
$$ {#eq-nn-update}
So $\mu\mid x \sim \mathcal N(\mu_n,\tau_n^2)$. Two facts make these formulas memorable, and
both are the kind of thing an interviewer loves:

- **Precisions add.** The posterior precision is the data precision $n/\sigma^2$ *plus* the
  prior precision $1/\tau_0^2$. Information, measured as precision, is additive — combining
  the prior with the data makes you strictly more certain (smaller posterior variance) than
  either alone. This is the Gaussian echo of the Fisher-information additivity from
  [INFR-B3].
- **The posterior mean is a precision-weighted average.** Dividing the $\mu_n$ formula
  through shows it is $\mu_n = \frac{(n/\sigma^2)\bar x + (1/\tau_0^2)\mu_0}{n/\sigma^2 +
  1/\tau_0^2}$ — each source pulls the centre in proportion to *how precise it is*. The same
  shrinkage story as the coin: data with high precision (large $n$) dominate; a vague prior
  (large $\tau_0^2$, tiny precision) barely tugs.

As $n\to\infty$, the data precision $n/\sigma^2$ swamps the prior precision, $\tau_n^2\to
\sigma^2/n$, and $\mu_n\to\bar x$: the posterior becomes a Gaussian centred at the MLE with
the frequentist standard error as its spread. **This is the precise sense in which the
Bayesian and frequentist answers merge with enough data** — a point we cash out
numerically in @sec-vs.

# The credible interval: the payoff {#sec-credible}

This is what the whole track was building toward — the interval that means what everyone
*wishes* a confidence interval meant.

::: {#def-credible}
**Definition (credible interval).** A **$(1-\alpha)$ credible interval** for $\theta$ is any
interval $[a,b]$ whose **posterior** probability is $1-\alpha$:
$$
P(a\le\theta\le b\mid x) \;=\; \int_a^b p(\theta\mid x)\,d\theta \;=\; 1-\alpha.
$$
Because $\theta$ is a random variable with the posterior as its distribution, this is a
*literal probability statement about $\theta$*.
:::

That last sentence is the entire philosophical payoff. Recall the cardinal forbidden
sentence from [INFR-C1]: "there is a $95\%$ probability that $\mu$ is in this interval."
For a confidence interval that sentence is false, because $\mu$ is fixed and the interval is
the only random thing. For a credible interval **the sentence is exactly true**, because we
*defined* $\theta$ to be random and the interval was carved to hold $95\%$ of its posterior
mass. The Bayesian turn bought us the interpretation the frequentist had to refuse.

There is a small but real ambiguity to resolve: many intervals hold $95\%$ of the mass.
Two conventions dominate.

- **Equal-tailed interval (ETI):** chop $\alpha/2$ off each tail — the interval from the
  $\alpha/2$ to the $1-\alpha/2$ posterior quantile. Easy, and invariant under monotone
  reparameterization.
- **Highest posterior density (HPD) interval:** the *shortest* interval with mass
  $1-\alpha$; equivalently, every point inside it has higher posterior density than every
  point outside. It is the most honest "where the belief concentrates," but harder to
  compute and not reparameterization-invariant.

For a symmetric, unimodal posterior (like the Normal–Normal) the two coincide, and it is
worth seeing why rather than taking it on faith. Symmetry about the mode $m$ means the two
equal-tailed quantiles sit at $m\pm c$ for the same offset $c$, and unimodality means the
density falls monotonically as you move away from $m$ in either direction; so the
equal-tailed endpoints $m\pm c$ have *equal* density (by symmetry) and every interior point
has strictly higher density (by unimodality) — which is exactly the defining property of the
HPD interval. Hence the equal-tailed interval already *is* the highest-density one. For a
skewed posterior (like a Beta near a boundary) symmetry fails: the two equal-tail endpoints
have different densities, so trimming equal mass from each tail is no longer the shortest
interval, and the ETI and HPD differ — the HPD is the tighter, more defensible summary.
@fig-credible shows both on the skewed coin posterior.

```{python}
#| label: fig-credible
#| fig-cap: 'Two 95% credible intervals on the same Beta(11,5) coin posterior (the navy curve from @fig-update). The equal-tailed interval (orange, dashed) cuts 2.5% of posterior mass off each tail. The highest-posterior-density interval (green) is the shortest interval containing 95% of the mass; on this right-skewed posterior it sits slightly left of the ETI and is narrower. Both are genuine probability statements: there is a 95% posterior probability that $\theta$ lies in either band. The shaded region is the HPD mass.'
post = stats.beta(11, 5)
theta = np.linspace(0, 1, 1000)
pdf = post.pdf(theta)

# Equal-tailed: the 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles.
eti_lo, eti_hi = post.ppf(0.025), post.ppf(0.975)

# HPD: scan a horizontal density threshold until the included mass is 95%.
def hpd_interval(dist, grid, mass=0.95):
    d = dist.pdf(grid)
    order = np.argsort(d)[::-1]            # densest points first
    csum = np.cumsum(dist.pdf(grid[order]) * (grid[1] - grid[0]))
    keep = grid[order][csum <= mass]
    return keep.min(), keep.max()

hpd_lo, hpd_hi = hpd_interval(post, theta)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(theta, pdf, color=POST, lw=2.4, label="posterior Beta(11,5)")
hmask = (theta >= hpd_lo) & (theta <= hpd_hi)
ax.fill_between(theta[hmask], pdf[hmask], color=FILL, alpha=0.5,
                label="95% HPD mass")
ax.axvline(eti_lo, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.6)
ax.axvline(eti_hi, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.6,
           label=f"equal-tailed [{eti_lo:.3f}, {eti_hi:.3f}]")
ax.axvline(hpd_lo, color=LIKE, lw=1.6)
ax.axvline(hpd_hi, color=LIKE, lw=1.6,
           label=f"HPD [{hpd_lo:.3f}, {hpd_hi:.3f}]")
ax.set_xlabel(r"coin bias $\theta$"); ax.set_ylabel("posterior density")
ax.set_title("Equal-tailed vs. highest-density credible intervals")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"Equal-tailed 95% credible interval: [{eti_lo:.4f}, {eti_hi:.4f}], width {eti_hi-eti_lo:.4f}")
print(f"HPD          95% credible interval: [{hpd_lo:.4f}, {hpd_hi:.4f}], width {hpd_hi-hpd_lo:.4f}")
```

## Credible interval vs. confidence interval, side by side {#sec-vs}

We now put the two intervals on the *same* Normal-mean problem so the difference is concrete
and not just philosophical. From [INFR-C1], the $95\%$ **confidence** interval for a Normal
mean with known variance is $\bar x \pm z_{\alpha/2}\,\sigma/\sqrt n$. From @eq-nn-update,
the $95\%$ **credible** interval is $\mu_n \pm z_{\alpha/2}\,\tau_n$ (the posterior is
Gaussian, so its equal-tailed interval is mean $\pm\, z$ posterior-SDs).

```{python}
#| label: fig-ci-vs-credible
#| fig-cap: 'Confidence interval vs. credible interval for a Normal mean ($\sigma=4$ known, true $\mu=10$). **Left, $n=8$:** with little data the prior still matters — a vague prior (Normal(0, $10^2$), green) gives a credible interval almost identical to the frequentist CI (red), while a confident-but-wrong prior centred at 5 (orange) shifts and shrinks its credible interval, visibly pulling the estimate toward the prior. **Right, $n=200$:** with ample data both priors'' credible intervals collapse onto the confidence interval — the data have overwhelmed the prior, and the two paradigms give numerically identical answers. The numbers agree in the large-sample limit; the *interpretation* never does.'
sigma, mu_true = 4.0, 10.0
z = 1.959963984540054

def intervals(n, seed):
    r = np.random.default_rng(seed)
    x = r.normal(mu_true, sigma, size=n)
    xbar = x.mean()
    # Frequentist CI (known sigma).
    ci = (xbar - z * sigma / np.sqrt(n), xbar + z * sigma / np.sqrt(n))
    out = {"CI": ci, "xbar": xbar}
    # Two Bayesian credible intervals (known sigma), priors N(mu0, tau0^2).
    for name, (mu0, tau0) in {"vague N(0,10^2)": (0.0, 10.0),
                              "tight N(5,1^2)": (5.0, 1.0)}.items():
        prec = n / sigma**2 + 1 / tau0**2
        taun = np.sqrt(1 / prec)
        mun = taun**2 * (n * xbar / sigma**2 + mu0 / tau0**2)
        out[name] = (mun - z * taun, mun + z * taun)
    return out

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 3.8), sharex=True)
for ax, n, title in [(axL, 8, "small sample (n=8)"),
                     (axR, 200, "large sample (n=200)")]:
    res = intervals(n, seed=n)
    rows = [("frequentist CI", res["CI"], TRUTH),
            ("credible, vague prior", res["vague N(0,10^2)"], LIKE),
            ("credible, tight wrong prior", res["tight N(5,1^2)"], ACCENT)]
    for i, (lab, (lo, hi), c) in enumerate(rows):
        ax.plot([lo, hi], [i, i], color=c, lw=3, solid_capstyle="round")
        ax.plot([(lo + hi) / 2], [i], "o", color=c, ms=6)
        ax.text((lo + hi) / 2, i + 0.18, lab, ha="center", fontsize=8, color=c)
    ax.axvline(mu_true, color=GREY, ls=":", lw=1.4)
    ax.set_ylim(-0.6, 2.8); ax.set_yticks([])
    ax.set_xlabel(r"$\mu$"); ax.set_title(title)
plt.tight_layout()
res8 = intervals(8, 8); res200 = intervals(200, 200)
print("n=8 : CI            ", tuple(round(v, 2) for v in res8["CI"]))
print("n=8 : credible vague", tuple(round(v, 2) for v in res8["vague N(0,10^2)"]))
print("n=8 : credible tight", tuple(round(v, 2) for v in res8["tight N(5,1^2)"]))
print("n=200: CI           ", tuple(round(v, 2) for v in res200["CI"]))
print("n=200: cred. vague  ", tuple(round(v, 2) for v in res200["vague N(0,10^2)"]))
print("n=200: cred. tight  ", tuple(round(v, 2) for v in res200["tight N(5,1^2)"]))
```

The picture tells the whole story of the two paradigms' relationship. With a **vague** prior
the credible interval is numerically almost identical to the confidence interval even at
small $n$ — which is reassuring and also a warning: a frequentist who quotes a CI is, to a
good approximation, a Bayesian with a flat prior who has not said so. With a **confident but
wrong** prior, the small-$n$ credible interval is visibly shifted and shrunk toward the
prior — the Bayesian estimate is *biased toward the prior*, which is a feature (you injected
real outside knowledge) or a bug (you injected a wrong assumption), depending entirely on
whether the prior was justified. And with **large $n$** every interval coincides: the data
swamp the prior and the two paradigms return the same numbers. The interpretations,
however, stay forever different — the CI is a statement about the procedure, the credible
interval a statement about $\theta$.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"If the numbers usually agree, why does the distinction matter?"* Three reasons, and a
strong candidate gives all three. **(1) Interpretation:** only the credible interval
licenses "$95\%$ probability $\theta$ is in here," which is the statement decision-makers
actually want; with a CI you must use the procedure language. **(2) Small samples and strong
priors:** when data are scarce and you genuinely know something (a physical bound, a
well-studied effect), the prior contributes real information and the intervals *diverge* —
the Bayesian one is often shorter and better-calibrated *if the prior is right*. **(3)
Awkward parameters:** for bounded or constrained parameters (a probability near 0 or 1, a
variance), the frequentist Wald interval can stray outside the parameter space or undercover
([INFR-C1]'s polling disaster, [INFR-C3]), whereas a posterior built on a prior supported on
the legal range *cannot*. The honest closing line: they agree when the prior is weak and
the sample large, and that is exactly the regime where the distinction is least interesting.
:::

# MAP estimation: the bridge to the MLE and to regularization {#sec-map}

A full posterior is the complete Bayesian answer, but often we still want a single number.
The **posterior mean** is one choice (it minimizes expected squared error). The other, which
connects beautifully back to the rest of the track and to Regression, is the **maximum a
posteriori** estimate.

::: {#def-map}
**Definition (MAP estimate).** The **maximum a posteriori** estimate is the value of
$\theta$ that maximizes the posterior — the posterior's **mode**:
$$
\hat\theta_{\text{MAP}} \;=\; \arg\max_\theta\; p(\theta\mid x)
\;=\; \arg\max_\theta\; p(x\mid\theta)\,\pi(\theta),
$$
where the marginal likelihood $p(x)$ drops because it is a positive constant in $\theta$ and
cannot move the location of a maximum.
:::

As in [INFR-B1] and [INFR-B2], we maximize in the log, because the log is strictly
increasing (it never moves the argmax) and it turns the product into a sum:
$$
\hat\theta_{\text{MAP}}
= \arg\max_\theta\;\Bigl[\,\underbrace{\log p(x\mid\theta)}_{\text{log-likelihood }\ell(\theta)}
                          \;+\; \log\pi(\theta)\,\Bigr].
$$ {#eq-map-log}
Compare this to the MLE, which maximizes $\ell(\theta)$ alone ([INFR-B2]). **The MAP is the
MLE with one extra additive term, $\log\pi(\theta)$ — a penalty (or reward) supplied by the
prior.** Two consequences follow immediately and are exactly what an interviewer fishes for.

## MAP reduces to MLE under a flat prior {#sec-map-flat}

If the prior is **flat** — $\pi(\theta)=c$, a constant, over the whole parameter range —
then $\log\pi(\theta)=\log c$ is itself a constant in $\theta$, and a constant added to a
function never moves its maximum. So @eq-map-log becomes $\arg\max_\theta \ell(\theta)$,
which is precisely the MLE:
$$
\pi(\theta)\propto 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \hat\theta_{\text{MAP}} = \hat\theta_{\text{MLE}}.
$$
This is the formal proof of the slogan from @sec-intuition: **the MLE is the MAP under a
flat prior**, i.e. the frequentist point estimate is the Bayesian one when you claim total
prior ignorance. (We saw the special case in the coin: the Beta$(1,1)$ flat prior makes the
posterior $\propto L(\theta)=\theta^k(1-\theta)^{n-k}$; maximizing it sets the derivative of
$\log L=k\log\theta+(n-k)\log(1-\theta)$ to zero, $k/\theta-(n-k)/(1-\theta)=0$, which solves
to $\theta=k/n$ — the binomial MLE of [INFR-B2]. So the posterior mode is $k/n$, the MLE.)

## MAP under a Gaussian prior is ridge; under a Laplace prior is lasso {#sec-map-reg}

This is the link the track roadmap promised back to Regression, and it is worth deriving in
full because it reveals **regularization to be Bayesian inference in disguise.** Consider the
linear-regression setting where the data log-likelihood, under Gaussian noise with variance
$\sigma^2$, is (from [REGR-01]'s Gaussian-MLE argument, which we cited there and use here)
$$
\ell(\boldsymbol\beta)
= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n\bigl(y_i - \mathbf x_i^\top\boldsymbol\beta\bigr)^2
  \;+\; \text{const},
$$
so maximizing $\ell$ is minimizing the sum of squared errors — ordinary least squares. Now
put a **mean-zero Gaussian prior** on each coefficient, $\beta_j\sim\mathcal N(0,\tau^2)$
independently, expressing a prior belief that coefficients are small. Its log is
$$
\log\pi(\boldsymbol\beta)
= -\frac{1}{2\tau^2}\sum_{j=1}^p \beta_j^2 \;+\; \text{const}.
$$
Substitute both into @eq-map-log. Maximizing the sum is the same as minimizing its negative;
multiply through by $-2\sigma^2$ (a positive constant, which does not move the minimizer) to
clear the leading factor:
$$
\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}_{\text{MAP}}
= \arg\min_{\boldsymbol\beta}\;
  \sum_{i=1}^n\bigl(y_i - \mathbf x_i^\top\boldsymbol\beta\bigr)^2
  \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{\sigma^2}{\tau^2}}_{\displaystyle\lambda}\sum_{j=1}^p \beta_j^2 .
$$ {#eq-ridge}
That is **ridge regression** exactly — least squares plus an $\ell_2$ penalty $\lambda\sum
\beta_j^2$, with the penalty strength $\lambda=\sigma^2/\tau^2$. The penalty is large
(strong shrinkage) when the prior is tight ($\tau^2$ small, you strongly believe
coefficients are near zero) and vanishes as $\tau^2\to\infty$ (a vague prior, recovering
OLS). The ridge penalty is not an arbitrary trick to tame overfitting; **it is the
log-density of a Gaussian prior on the coefficients.**

Swap the Gaussian prior for a **Laplace (double-exponential) prior**, $\pi(\beta_j)\propto
\exp(-|\beta_j|/b)$, whose log is $-|\beta_j|/b + \text{const}$. The identical substitution
gives
$$
\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}_{\text{MAP}}
= \arg\min_{\boldsymbol\beta}\;
  \sum_{i=1}^n\bigl(y_i - \mathbf x_i^\top\boldsymbol\beta\bigr)^2
  \;+\; \lambda\sum_{j=1}^p |\beta_j|,
$$ {#eq-lasso}
which is **lasso regression** — least squares plus an $\ell_1$ penalty. The Laplace prior's
sharp peak at zero is what drives coefficients *exactly* to zero (the sparsity the lasso is
famous for). @fig-priors-penalty shows the two priors and the penalties they induce.

```{python}
#| label: fig-priors-penalty
#| fig-cap: 'Regularization is a prior in disguise. **Left:** a Gaussian prior (green) and a Laplace prior (orange) on a single coefficient, both centred at zero. The Laplace has a sharp peak at zero and heavier tails. **Right:** the corresponding penalties $-\log\pi(\beta)$ that the MAP adds to the least-squares loss: the Gaussian''s $\beta^2$ (ridge) and the Laplace''s $|\beta|$ (lasso). The lasso penalty''s corner at zero is exactly what pushes coefficients to be *exactly* zero, producing sparse fits; the smooth ridge penalty only shrinks them. The penalty you choose in Regression is the prior you believe in Inference.'
b = np.linspace(-3, 3, 400)
gauss = np.exp(-b**2 / 2) / np.sqrt(2 * np.pi)
laplace = np.exp(-np.abs(b)) / 2

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 3.7))
axL.plot(b, gauss, color=LIKE, lw=2.2, label="Gaussian prior")
axL.plot(b, laplace, color=ACCENT, lw=2.2, label="Laplace prior")
axL.set_title("Priors on a coefficient")
axL.set_xlabel(r"$\beta$"); axL.set_ylabel("prior density")
axL.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axR.plot(b, b**2, color=LIKE, lw=2.2, label=r"ridge penalty $\beta^2$ (Gaussian)")
axR.plot(b, np.abs(b), color=ACCENT, lw=2.2, label=r"lasso penalty $|\beta|$ (Laplace)")
axR.set_title(r"Induced penalty $-\log\pi(\beta)$")
axR.set_xlabel(r"$\beta$"); axR.set_ylabel("penalty")
axR.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is regularized regression Bayesian or frequentist?"* It can be read either way, and saying
so is the sophisticated answer. As a *frequentist* procedure, ridge/lasso is penalized
maximum likelihood — a point estimate that trades a little bias for a lot of variance
reduction, justified by its risk properties with no prior in sight. As a *Bayesian*
procedure, it is the **MAP estimate** under a Gaussian (ridge) or Laplace (lasso) prior on
the coefficients — the single point of @eq-ridge–@eq-lasso. The penalty strength $\lambda$ is
the frequentist's tuning knob and the Bayesian's prior variance ($\lambda=\sigma^2/\tau^2$);
they are the same number wearing two hats. The catch worth volunteering: the MAP is only the
*mode* of the posterior, so it throws away the rest of the distribution — a full Bayesian
analysis would report the whole posterior (and the posterior *mean*, not the mode, which
for the lasso is not sparse). Regression's own derivation of the ridge and lasso penalties,
at a different altitude, is in [REGR-08]–[REGR-09].
:::

# Bayesian hypothesis testing, briefly {#sec-testing}

To close the contrast with the testing movement (D), note how the same posterior re-answers
a test. In [INFR-D1] a frequentist test asks: *under $H_0$, how surprising are the data?* —
the p-value. The Bayesian asks the question decision-makers actually want: *given the data,
how probable is each hypothesis?* For a one-sided claim like "the effect is positive,"
$\theta>0$, the answer is a direct **posterior probability**,
$$
P(\theta > 0\mid x) = \int_0^\infty p(\theta\mid x)\,d\theta,
$$
read straight off the posterior — no null distribution, no tail-area-under-a-hypothesis
gymnastics. For comparing two models $M_0,M_1$, the Bayesian computes the **Bayes factor**
$p(x\mid M_1)/p(x\mid M_0)$, the ratio of marginal likelihoods @eq-marginal — which is
precisely where the troublesome integral becomes unavoidable, and which we flag as the
gateway to the computation track rather than develop here.

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

The Bayesian turn buys a clean interpretation at a real cost, and a graduate must be able to
state the costs as crisply as the benefits.

## Prior sensitivity: the same data, different conclusions {#sec-sensitivity}

The credible interval's honesty is conditional on the prior, and with little data the prior
can drive the answer. We make this an *honest* example — not one rigged to flatter Bayes,
but the regime where it strains.

```{python}
#| label: fig-prior-sensitivity
#| fig-cap: "The edge of applicability: prior sensitivity with little data. A rare-event count — 1 success in 10 trials — analysed under four Beta priors. **Top:** an enthusiast's prior (Beta(8,2), believing success is likely), a skeptic's (Beta(2,8)), a flat prior (Beta(1,1)), and Jeffreys (Beta(0.5,0.5)). **Bottom:** the four posteriors. With only $n=10$ they disagree substantially — the enthusiast's posterior mean is more than double the skeptic's, and the 95% credible intervals barely overlap. *This is the honest boundary*: when data are scarce relative to the strength of the prior, the prior, not the data, decides the answer, and a responsible analysis must report exactly this sensitivity table."
theta = np.linspace(0, 1, 500)
k, n = 1, 10
priors = {"enthusiast Beta(8,2)": (8, 2),
          "skeptic Beta(2,8)": (2, 8),
          "flat Beta(1,1)": (1, 1),
          "Jeffreys Beta(.5,.5)": (0.5, 0.5)}
colors = [LIKE, ACCENT, PRIOR, POST]

fig, (axT, axB) = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(6.6, 6.0), sharex=True)
for (name, (a, b)), c in zip(priors.items(), colors):
    axT.plot(theta, stats.beta(a, b).pdf(theta), color=c, lw=2.0, label=name)
    post = stats.beta(a + k, b + n - k)
    lo, hi = post.ppf(0.025), post.ppf(0.975)
    axB.plot(theta, post.pdf(theta), color=c, lw=2.2,
             label=f"mean {post.mean():.2f}, 95% CrI [{lo:.2f},{hi:.2f}]")
axT.set_title("Four priors"); axT.set_ylabel("density")
axT.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
axB.set_title(f"Four posteriors after {k} success in {n} trials")
axB.set_xlabel(r"$\theta$"); axB.set_ylabel("density")
axB.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print("Posterior summaries (k=1, n=10):")
for name, (a, b) in priors.items():
    post = stats.beta(a + k, b + n - k)
    print(f"  {name:24s} mean={post.mean():.3f}  95% CrI=[{post.ppf(0.025):.3f}, {post.ppf(0.975):.3f}]")
```

The lesson is not "Bayes is unreliable." It is that **with little data the prior is part of
the answer, and you must say which prior you used.** Frequentist inference dodges this by
refusing to use a prior — but it pays for the dodge with the inability to answer the direct
question, and (as [INFR-C1]'s polling example showed) its own small-sample intervals can
misbehave near boundaries. The honest practice on both sides: with scarce data, report the
sensitivity; with ample data, it does not matter (the posteriors in @fig-prior-sensitivity
would be indistinguishable at $n=1000$).

## When Bayes and frequentism agree, and when they diverge {#sec-agree}

Pulling the threads together into the applicability call a graduate must be able to make:

- **They agree (numerically) when** the prior is weak relative to the data — large $n$, a
  vague prior, a well-identified parameter. The Bernstein–von Mises phenomenon (the
  large-sample fact that the posterior becomes Gaussian centred at the MLE with the inverse
  Fisher information as variance — the [INFR-B4] limit reappearing) is the formal statement;
  we name it as an imported result rather than prove it, since its proof needs the
  asymptotic machinery of [INFR-B4] plus regularity conditions. Both intervals then have the
  same endpoints, and a $95\%$ credible interval also has $\approx 95\%$ frequentist
  coverage.
- **They diverge when** data are scarce and the prior is informative (the prior genuinely
  shifts the answer), when the parameter is on a boundary (a probability near 0/1, a
  variance near 0 — where the prior's support constraint helps and the frequentist Wald
  interval breaks), or when the question itself is inherently about a *single, unrepeatable*
  event ("what is the probability *this* patient has the disease given *this* test result"),
  which the frequentist frame cannot phrase as a probability at all.

## The computational price {#sec-computation}

Everything by hand above worked because the models were **conjugate** — the posterior had a
recognizable closed form and the integral @eq-marginal evaporated. The moment the prior and
likelihood are not conjugate — which is *most* real models — that integral is a
high-dimensional beast with no closed form, and even *evaluating* the normalized posterior,
let alone its credible intervals, requires numerical integration that is hopeless beyond a
few dimensions.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Conjugacy is the exception, not the rule.** The Beta–Binomial and Normal–Normal updates in
this article are clean because the algebra of the prior matched the algebra of the
likelihood. Almost any realistic model — logistic regression with a Gaussian prior, a
hierarchical model, anything with several interacting parameters — is **not** conjugate, and
the posterior is known only up to the intractable constant $p(x)$. You cannot integrate it,
and you often cannot even normalize it. The escape is to **sample** from the posterior
without ever computing $p(x)$ — Markov chain Monte Carlo (Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs,
Hamiltonian Monte Carlo) and variational inference. That machinery is large enough to be its
own track; this article deliberately stops at the conjugate edge so that the *concepts* —
posterior, credible interval, MAP — are rock-solid before the computation is layered on.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When would you actually choose Bayesian over frequentist inference in practice?"* The
practitioner's checklist: **reach for Bayes** when (a) you have genuine, defensible prior
information and the data are limited, so the prior earns its keep; (b) the decision-maker
needs a *direct* probability about the parameter or a hypothesis (medical risk,
business-decision expected loss) rather than a long-run-frequency guarantee; (c) the model is
hierarchical or has many partially-pooled groups, where Bayesian shrinkage is natural and
coherent; or (d) the parameter is constrained and frequentist intervals misbehave. **Stay
frequentist** when (a) you want a guarantee that needs no prior and is calibrated over
repetition (regulatory settings, A/B tests at scale where the procedure's long-run error
rate is the contract); (b) priors would be contentious and you want assumption-light
answers (reach for the bootstrap, [INFR-E1]); or (c) you simply have so much data that the
two agree and the frequentist tooling is faster. The naive answer picks a side; the graduate
answer matches the tool to the question.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
This article **closes** the Statistical Inference track. It kept the charter question — *how
sure are we* — and answered it under a different definition of probability, treating the
parameter as random.

- It assumed **[INFR-B1 *The Likelihood*]** (the posterior is the likelihood times a prior),
  **[INFR-C1 *Confidence Intervals*]** (the foil for the credible interval — the cardinal
  forbidden sentence that the credible interval *is allowed* to say), and **[INFR-D1 *The
  Logic of Hypothesis Testing*]** (the testing apparatus we re-answered with posterior
  probabilities and Bayes factors).
- It reached back to **Regression**: the **MAP** estimate under a Gaussian prior is **ridge**
  ([REGR-08]) and under a Laplace prior is **lasso** ([REGR-09]) — regularization revealed as
  a prior in disguise. The Gaussian-noise likelihood it borrowed is from [REGR-01].
- It reused the large-sample machinery of **[INFR-B4 *MLE Asymptotics*]**: the
  Bernstein–von Mises phenomenon (posterior $\to$ Gaussian at the MLE with inverse Fisher
  information variance) is why Bayes and frequentism merge with enough data, and it pointed
  to **[INFR-B3 *Fisher Information*]** for the Jeffreys prior.
- It **forks to a future Bayesian Computation / MCMC track.** The conjugate cases here are
  the entire hand-computable universe; the intractable marginal likelihood @eq-marginal is
  the wall that Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs sampling, Hamiltonian Monte Carlo, and
  variational inference are built to climb. Those methods need the posterior *concepts*
  installed here as their foundation.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The founding sources.** T. Bayes, "An Essay towards Solving a Problem in the Doctrine of
  Chances," *Phil. Trans.* (1763), and P.-S. Laplace, *Mémoire sur la probabilité des
  causes par les événements* (1774) — the latter is where the modern machinery actually
  takes shape, including the rule of succession from the @sec-beta-binom callout.
- **The modern graduate texts.** A. Gelman et al., *Bayesian Data Analysis* (3rd ed.) — the
  standard reference, with conjugate analysis, prior choice, and the computation that
  follows this article; and C. Robert, *The Bayesian Choice*, for the decision-theoretic
  foundations (why the posterior mean minimizes expected squared loss, the MAP its own
  loss).
- **The objective-prior thread.** H. Jeffreys' prior, $\pi(\theta)\propto\sqrt{I(\theta)}$
  built from the Fisher information of [INFR-B3], is the principled answer to "what does
  'know nothing' mean," and is reparameterization-invariant by construction — the fix for
  the subtlety flagged in the @sec-beta-binom callout.
- **Open threads deliberately deferred.** *How do we compute the posterior when it is not
  conjugate?* — MCMC and variational inference, the future computation track. *How do we
  compare whole models?* — Bayes factors and the marginal likelihood @eq-marginal, whose
  evaluation is the same hard integral. *Is there a prior that makes the credible interval
  have exact frequentist coverage?* — the matching-prior program, a deep corner where the two
  paradigms are engineered to coincide.
